title: Sally Lunn,Bath tea loaf
subtitle: Tall, rich, brioche-tender bread eaten in slabs with butter
excerpt: The Bath tea bread credited to the eighteenth-century baker Sally Lunn,an enriched yeasted loaf shaped tall in a deep tin, with a soft golden crumb that takes butter, clotted cream, or lemon curd at tea time. Half-brioche, half-bread; baked in one tin and torn open warm.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932),the Bath bun and Sally Lunn entries from her West Country chapter; the tall-tin shaping is hers. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136,the enriched tea-bread template (eggs, milk, sugar in moderate quantity). The Sally Lunn name appears in Bath baker's records from the 1780s; the recipe here follows the soft, lightly-sweet West Country reading rather than the harder, more bun-like London version.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 tall round loaf (15 cm tin, serves 8 to 10)
recipe.freezeNotes: Cool fully, wrap tightly, and freeze for up to 3 months. Defrost wrapped at room temperature; warm in a low oven for 8 minutes before slicing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix the dough late afternoon, prove slow, and bake the next morning by shifting the second rise to the fridge overnight (8 to 12 hours). Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C. The deep tin holds heat, so a moderate temperature lets the centre cook through before the top darkens.

recipeTools.notes: With dough hook. The dough is rich and benefits from the steady kneading but hand-kneading works.
Or a deep 15 cm round tin,taller than a standard cake tin.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bread dough with significant fat, sugar, eggs, or dairy added beyond the basic flour-water-yeast-salt structure. The fat coats the gluten strands, so the crumb is softer and the rise is slower than a lean dough. Sally Lunn, brioche, panettone, and challah are all enriched. Expect the bulk ferment to take longer than a plain white loaf,judge by volume, not the clock.

content.text: Sally Lunn sits between bread and brioche. The crumb is soft and pale, the crust thin and pale gold, and the tall shape comes from baking the lot in one deep tin rather than rolling it into buns. One loaf takes about 25 minutes of active work, two rises across the afternoon, and 30 minutes in the oven. Torn open warm and spread with butter, it is the classic Bath tea bread,and equally good toasted the next day under lemon curd or clotted cream.

, so the rise will be slower than a plain tin loaf. Read the dough, not the clock,judge each rise by how much volume the dough has gained, not by minutes elapsed. The kitchen temperature matters. A cold kitchen will extend the first rise by an hour or more.

items.prepNote: for the egg-wash, mixed with 1 tsp milk

content.text: Whisk the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar together in the bowl of a stand mixer, keeping the salt and yeast on opposite sides until you mix. Pour in the warm milk and beaten eggs and combine on a low speed with the dough hook for 2 minutes, until a rough dough comes together. Stop the mixer and add the soft butter a tablespoon at a time, with the mixer running on low between additions. The dough will slacken, then come back together,keep going until each piece is absorbed before adding the next. By hand: tip out and knead the butter in piece by piece on the bench, scraping and folding.

Knead for 8 minutes in the stand mixer on medium speed, or 12 to 15 minutes by hand, until the dough is smooth, soft, and elastic. It should pull cleanly from the sides of the bowl and pass the windowpane test,stretched thin between your fingers, it should hold without tearing. Enriched dough takes longer to develop than a lean dough; do not skip the kneading.

Lightly oil a clean bowl, transfer the dough, and cover with a damp cloth or cling film. Leave to rise in a warm place for 90 minutes, until visibly doubled. The enriched dough rises gently; if your kitchen is cool, allow up to 2 hours and trust the volume rather than the clock.

Butter a deep 15 cm round tin generously. Tip the risen dough onto a lightly floured surface and knock it back gently,just enough to expel the largest pockets of gas. Shape into a tight round by tucking the edges underneath and rotating against the bench until the surface is smooth. Drop the ball, seam-side down, into the tin and press gently to fit. The dough should fill about half the tin's depth.

Cover loosely and prove for 60 minutes, until the dough has risen to within 2 cm of the rim and feels soft and puffed. A finger pressed lightly into the side should leave a slow-rebounding indent,not a snap-back, not a permanent dent.

Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Brush the surface gently with the egg-yolk wash, reaching into the crevices around the edge of the tin. Bake on the middle shelf for 30 minutes, until the top is deep gold, the sides have shrunk slightly from the tin, and an instant-read thermometer pushed into the centre reads 90°C. If the top darkens too fast at 20 minutes, tent with foil for the last 10.

Tip out onto a wire rack and cool for at least 20 minutes before slicing. Sally Lunn is best torn rather than cut, into thick wedges, and eaten warm with cold butter. Day-two: split horizontally, toast under the grill, and top with clotted cream and strawberry jam.
